markdown:
Both numbers are correct — they refer to different scopes, not a genuine contradiction.

## The two claims, side by side

**Adam's recommended default (β₂ = 0.999)** — Kingma & Ba's Algorithm 1 caption states:

> "Good default settings for the tested machine learning problems are α = 0.001, β₁ = 0.9, **β₂ = 0.999** and ε = 10⁻⁸." [1]

Note the qualifier: these are defaults "for the tested machine learning problems" (logistic regression, multilayer MLP on MNIST, and a convolutional network). The value is an empirically chosen default on those small-scale benchmarks, not a universal prescription.

**Transformer's chosen value (β₂ = 0.98)** — Vaswani et al. (§5.3 Optimizer) state:

> "We used the Adam optimizer [20] with β₁ = 0.9, **β₂ = 0.98** and ε = 10⁻⁹." [2]

This is the authors' configuration for training the Transformer base/big models on WMT 2014 machine translation.

## Resolution

There is no actual conflict — the scope differs:

| | β₂ | ε | Scope |
|---|---|---|---|
| Adam paper | 0.999 | 10⁻⁸ | Recommendation for the small-scale problems tested in Adam |
| Transformer paper | 0.98 | 10⁻⁹ | Empirical choice for Transformer training |

The Transformer paper explicitly cites Adam [2], so its authors knowingly depart from Kingma & Ba's default. Smaller β₂ values shorten the effective window of the second-moment moving average, which can matter when gradients are sparse or noisy in ways the original Adam benchmarks did not exercise. (The Transformer passage does not itself justify the choice — only states it.)

**Bottom line:** 0.999 is the Adam default; 0.98 is a task-specific override used by the Transformer authors. Both are true within their respective scopes.

Sources
[1] Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization, unsectioned, p. 2
[2] Attention Is All You Need, 5.3 Optimizer, p. 7
